metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that defines what 'release evidence' means (e.g., a workflow-summary entry, a GitHub Release body field, or a committed file) and verifies that the approved SHA, version, manifest versions, and artifact hashes appear there after a successful dispatch run.
findings.finding: The 'What' section describes a concrete deliverable — 'Record the approved SHA, version, manifest versions, and artifact hashes in release evidence' — but no AC covers it: none of AC1–AC8 verifies that this artifact/record exists, where it is written, or what 'release evidence' means (a workflow summary, a file committed to the repo, a GitHub Release body field, etc.).
findings.location: What section, last bullet ('Record the approved SHA…'); Acceptance Criteria AC1–AC8

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit named fix in the plan's Scope / What section: replace `TAG: ${{ github.ref_name }}` in the `mcp_registry` job with `TAG: ${{ github.event.inputs.<version-input-name> }}` (or the equivalent dispatch input), and note this as a required change alongside the trigger switch — verified by confirming the `Wait for PyPI` step polls the correct version URL on a dispatch run.
findings.finding: The `mcp_registry` job's version extraction uses `TAG: ${{ github.ref_name }}` (release.yml:141, 160) and `version="${TAG#v}"`. Under the current `push: tags: v*` trigger, `github.ref_name` resolves to `v0.7.1`. After the plan's change to `workflow_dispatch` from `main`, `github.ref_name` resolves to `main`, making `version="${TAG#v}"` = `ain` — silently wrong. The plan asserts the MCP job 'derives the version from the dispatch input' (correct fix direction), but does not name this as a concrete breaking change in the mcp_registry job that must be addressed, nor does it specify which input variable replaces `github.ref_name`.
findings.location: What section — 'Tag + GitHub Release created AFTER a successful publish' / 'The MCP job derives the version from the dispatch input'; release.yml:141, 160
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section under `.github/workflows/release.yml`, add: 'Replace `TAG: ${{ github.ref_name }}` in the `mcp_registry` job (lines 141, 160) with `TAG: v${{ github.event.inputs.<version_input_name> }}` so version extraction works under `workflow_dispatch`; also update the `Wait for PyPI` URL accordingly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the conflict between the leaf's 'No raw-tag publish path' decision and the parent epic's Success Criterion requiring 'a retained `v*` tag-push fallback additionally asserts ancestry to `origin/main`': either update the parent SC to reflect the stronger 'eliminate the tag-push publish path entirely' decision (which re-reviews the parent), or realign the leaf to retain a tag-push fallback with an ancestry assertion added to it.
findings.finding: The parent epic's Success Criteria for Finding 1 explicitly requires 'a retained `v*` tag-push fallback additionally asserts ancestry to `origin/main`', but the leaf plan eliminates the tag-push publish path entirely ('No raw-tag publish path' bullet; AC5 verifies a hand-pushed v* tag cannot publish). This is a direct contradiction: the parent requires retention with an added ancestry guard; the leaf removes it. Under the conflict rule the parent wins.
findings.location: Leaf plan 'What' section — 'No raw-tag publish path' bullet; Leaf AC5; Parent epic Success Criteria paragraph 1 (Finding 1 outcome)
findings.suggested_fix: If the stronger 'no fallback' approach is correct (which is architecturally cleaner), update the parent epic's SC to read 'the `v*` tag-push publish path is eliminated; publication is only possible via a dispatch-from-`main`, reviewer-approved run' — then re-review the parent before re-reviewing this leaf.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the evidence-record target in the Scope section (e.g., a workflow step in `release.yml` that writes a summary to `$GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY` or uploads a named artifact) and add a corresponding AC that verifies its presence in a successful run.
findings.finding: The 'What' section contains a behavioral hunk — 'Record the approved SHA, version, manifest versions, and artifact hashes in release evidence' — that has no corresponding file target in the Scope section and no matching Acceptance Criterion, leaving the implementation target ambiguous (workflow step? separate artifact? docs section? a new file?).
findings.location: What section (last bullet); Scope section; Acceptance Criteria

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC3) requiring at least one end-to-end successful dispatch-triggered publish to TestPyPI (or a staging environment with a registered trusted publisher), confirming the OIDC token exchange, the re-keyed environment gate, and the post-publish tag creation all succeed in the actual dispatch-from-main context — not just that the workflow YAML is structured correctly.
findings.finding: AC3 verifies the new direct-OIDC-publish code path only by static inspection of the workflow file ('single workflow_dispatch job graph; no push: tags publish trigger'), not by an actual end-to-end publish execution. Per E6(f), cutting over from the tag-relay trigger to a direct dispatch-triggered publish requires at least one AC that EXERCISES the publish path as it will run (e.g., a successful TestPyPI publish from a dispatch run), because green static analysis of the YAML does not prove the OIDC token exchange, the trusted-publisher registration, or the job-ordering constraints actually work end-to-end.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 ('The workflow builds and then publishes directly via the OIDC trusted-publishing flow...')
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3: '…verified (against a staging TestPyPI target with a trusted publisher registered under the dispatch/main context) by a complete dispatch run that successfully publishes a test artifact, then creates the v* tag — confirming OIDC subject-claim matching, the re-keyed environment approval gate, and the post-publish ordering all work end-to-end.'

findings.finding: No acceptance criterion exercises the full happy-path of the new primary release flow end-to-end (dispatch → version-check passes → tag auto-created → build/publish triggered → artifact published). AC3 verifies the flow only by reading the workflow YAML (structural check), not by running a real dispatch that produces a real tag and a real (staging/TestPyPI) publication. This is a cutover to a new code path with no criterion that exercises the new path as it will run in production.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3; What section — two-phase flow
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '[ ] A workflow_dispatch run from `main` with matching versions completes end-to-end against TestPyPI/staging: the dispatch job creates the `v*` tag, the tag push triggers `build`/`publish`, and the publish job uploads a release artifact to TestPyPI (verified by the TestPyPI release URL in the job summary).' This satisfies E6(f) for the cutover path.

findings.finding: The new default path — `workflow_dispatch` from `main` successfully completing version-check + ancestry assertion, then creating and pushing the `v*` tag, which triggers the downstream build/publish chain — is verified in AC3 only by 'the workflow definition' (a structural/definitional check), not by a live or staged end-to-end run. This is a proxy-validation gap on the cutover path: the suite can go green while the dispatch → tag-push → downstream-trigger chain is broken in production.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3; 'Testing safely' section
findings.suggested_fix: Replace or augment AC3's 'verified by the workflow definition' clause with: 'AND verified by a successful dispatch run against the staging target where the workflow-created tag and the triggered downstream build job are both observable in the Actions run log (or equivalent staging evidence).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly call out in the `release.yml` Scope section that the `mcp_registry` job must replace `TAG: ${{ github.ref_name }}` / `version="${TAG#v}"` with the workflow dispatch input (e.g. `version: ${{ inputs.version }}`) since `github.ref_name` is the branch name in a dispatch context and the tag does not yet exist when `mcp_registry` runs.
findings.finding: The `mcp_registry` job's version-extraction logic (`version="${TAG#v}"` from `github.ref_name`) silently breaks under the new design: in a `workflow_dispatch` context, `github.ref_name` is the branch name ('main'), not a tag, so `version` evaluates to 'main' rather than the release version. The plan's What section mentions 'The MCP job derives the version from the dispatch input (not from a tag)' but the Scope section's description of `release.yml` changes does not name this as an explicit required edit, making it an implementation trap.
findings.location: What section — 'Tag + GitHub Release created AFTER a successful publish'; Scope section — '.github/workflows/release.yml'; current release.yml:141-143 (`TAG: ${{ github.ref_name }}`, `version="${TAG#v}"`)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section under `.github/workflows/release.yml`, add: 'Update the `mcp_registry` job to derive the version from `inputs.version` (the dispatch input) rather than `github.ref_name`, since the tag is created after publish and `github.ref_name` in a dispatch context is the branch name, not a version string.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Amend AC5 to explicitly state it is only satisfiable after AC7 is attested, e.g. add '(requires AC7 operator attestation to be on record before this AC is considered complete)' so the dependency is enforced by the acceptance process.
findings.finding: AC5 ('a hand-pushed v* tag cannot publish — verified by absence of a tag-triggered publish path in release.yml AND by docs/releasing.md') asserts a security property that depends on TWO independent controls: (a) no push:tags trigger in the workflow (static, checkable) and (b) the pypi environment deployment-ref policy being re-keyed (operator-attested, AC7). AC5 can be signed off from the workflow YAML alone, before AC7 is done, giving a false 'no raw-tag publish path' signal. The dependency is unstated.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5 ('A hand-pushed v* tag cannot publish…')
findings.suggested_fix: AC5 revised: '…A hand-pushed v* tag cannot publish — verified by (a) the absence of a push:tags publish trigger in release.yml, (b) docs/releasing.md documenting dispatch-only publication, AND (c) AC7 operator attestation on record confirming the live pypi environment is re-keyed; AC5 is not satisfiable before AC7 is attested.'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the reviewer-rejection and approval-timeout exit paths in `docs/releasing.md` (and reference them in the 'Human authorization' section of the plan): confirm no partial artifacts are emitted when the gate is not approved, and specify whether re-dispatching the same version input is safe or requires a version bump.
findings.finding: The required-reviewers gate is a core interactive authorization surface, but the plan covers only the happy path (reviewer approves → publish proceeds) and the pre-gate failure paths (wrong ref, version mismatch, non-ancestor SHA). The abandonment exit — a reviewer explicitly rejects the deployment, or the environment approval window times out — is not addressed: the plan does not tell operators what state is left, whether any partial artifacts were emitted, or how to safely re-initiate a release after a rejected/timed-out approval.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the pypi environment'; AC: 'A hand-pushed v* tag cannot publish'; docs/releasing.md scope item
findings.suggested_fix: Add to docs/releasing.md under the reviewer-requirement section: (1) If a reviewer rejects or the approval window expires, no publish or tag action has run — the workflow is safe to re-dispatch with the same version. (2) Confirm via the workflow graph that the 'publish' job (which creates side-effects) is strictly sequenced after the environment gate, so rejection leaves no partial state.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Codify the `pypi` environment's deployment-ref policy (restricted to `refs/heads/main` via `workflow_dispatch`) and required-reviewers setting as IaC (e.g., `github_repository_environment` + `github_repository_environment_deployment_policy` resources in `infra/terraform-github/main.tf`) so drift is caught by `terraform plan` and the control is reproducible as-code — or explicitly document why IaC management is infeasible and add a CI drift-detection check (e.g., a scheduled workflow asserting the environment's protection rules via the GitHub API).
findings.finding: The re-keyed `pypi` GitHub deployment environment — the plan's PRIMARY security control preventing arbitrary-tag publication — is explicitly left as an 'operator step (not code)' with no IaC representation, no drift detection, and no idempotency guarantee. A GitHub UI accident, environment recreation, or future automation could silently revert it to 'v* tags only', reinstating the exact vulnerability this plan is designed to close, with no automated signal.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Operator step (not code): re-key the pypi GitHub environment…'; AC7: '[operator-attested] The live pypi GitHub environment is re-keyed…'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Scope / operator step that the environment deployment-branch policy enforces only the *ref pattern* (`main`), and that 'dispatch-only' enforcement comes from removing `push: tags` from release.yml plus the `if: github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch'` guard — so both changes are required in tandem, not just the environment re-key.
findings.finding: The plan states the `pypi` GitHub environment should be re-keyed to '`main` via `workflow_dispatch`', implying the environment deployment-branch policy itself enforces the event-type restriction. GitHub's environment deployment_branch_policy only constrains which *ref patterns* can access the environment — it cannot restrict by event type (`workflow_dispatch` vs. `push`). The 'dispatch-only' enforcement actually comes from (a) removing the `push: tags` trigger from release.yml and (b) the `if: github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch'` guard — not from the environment policy. This is feasible, but if someone only re-keys the environment policy (without removing the push trigger), a `push` to `main` would bypass the intended event-type restriction.
findings.location: What section — 'Human authorization — re-key the `pypi` environment'; Scope — 'Operator step (not code): re-key the `pypi` GitHub environment's deployment-ref policy'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the operator step: 'GitHub environment deployment_branch_policy enforces the ref pattern (`main`) but NOT the event type; the dispatch-only gate is enforced by the workflow's removed `push: tags` trigger and the `if: github.event_name == "workflow_dispatch" && github.ref == "refs/heads/main"` guard in the job — both changes must land together.'

findings.finding: The dispatch job must push a git tag (`git push origin vX.Y.Z`), which requires `contents: write`. The current `release.yml` declares `permissions: contents: read` at the workflow level (line 23–24), and no job-level override is specified in the plan for the new dispatch job. Without a job-level `permissions: contents: write` override, the tag-push step will fail with a 403.
findings.location: Scope — `.github/workflows/release.yml`; What — Two-phase flow; current release.yml line 23–24
findings.suggested_fix: Add `permissions: contents: write` as a job-level key on the new dispatch/version-check job in `release.yml`, mirroring the pattern already used on the `github_release` job (line 83).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the version input format convention in the plan (e.g. 'bare semver `X.Y.Z`; the workflow prepends `v` when creating the Git tag and GitHub Release') so the executor does not need to make this choice.
findings.finding: The workflow_dispatch version input format is not specified (v-prefixed semver 'v1.2.3' vs bare semver '1.2.3'). The version-lockstep check asserts `version_input == pyproject.toml version`, but pyproject.toml conventionally stores bare semver ('1.2.3') while Git tags use the v-prefix ('v1.2.3'). Without a stated convention an executor must choose, risking a permanent false-mismatch failure or a silent tag-name divergence.
findings.location: What — 'Version-lockstep, before build'; Acceptance Criteria — AC2 ('version_input == project.version == …')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Version-lockstep, before build' bullet: 'The dispatch input accepts bare semver (e.g. `1.2.3`); the version-check step compares it directly against pyproject.toml and server.json (also bare); the workflow prefixes `v` only when creating the Git tag and GitHub Release name.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the two irreversible steps (removing the push:tags trigger from release.yml and re-keying the pypi environment), specifying either an expand-contract sequence (run both trigger paths in parallel until the dispatch path is validated) or the exact operator steps to restore the previous state if the new path fails.
findings.finding: The plan removes the push:tags:v* publish trigger and re-keys the pypi GitHub environment (both irreversible/destructive changes for the release pipeline), but provides no rollback or back-out plan. T4 requires an explicit reversibility mechanism, not just acknowledgement of the change.
findings.location: What section ('No raw-tag publish path'; 'Operator step (not code): re-key the pypi GitHub environment'); Scope section; AC7
findings.suggested_fix: Consider an expand-contract sequence: (1) add the workflow_dispatch trigger and dispatch-based jobs while keeping the existing push:tags path temporarily; (2) validate a full dispatch-based release against TestPyPI; (3) only then remove the push:tags trigger and re-key the environment. Document the revert steps (restore push:tags in release.yml, re-key environment back to v* tags) in docs/releasing.md under a 'Rollback' subsection.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Create a durable in-repo decision record (e.g., `docs/adr/release-dispatch-from-main.md` or a dedicated section in `CLAUDE.md`) capturing: the tag-relay gap, the alternatives considered (tag-triggered vs. dispatch-from-main), the no-tag-lock trade-off, and the environment re-key rationale — so the 'why' survives ticket closure.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a significant novel architectural decision — dispatch-from-main as the release source of truth, no-tag-relay, ancestry assertion as defense-in-depth, environment re-key strategy — but does not earmark creation of an ADR or a CLAUDE.md update. The rationale lives only in the plan/issue document, not in any durable in-repository decision record. Once the story closes, future maintainers can read the runbook in docs/releasing.md but cannot easily find the 'why' (the tag-relay gap, alternatives considered, the deliberate no-tag-lock trade-off).
findings.location: Scope section / docs/releasing.md bullet; T5e(c)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an ADR under docs/adr/ (or a 'Decision record' section in CLAUDE.md) with: Context (tag-relay gap, PEP 740 limitation), Decision (dispatch-from-main + environment re-key), Consequences (no raw-tag publish path, operator environment step required), and Alternatives Rejected (keep tag-triggered but add ancestry check — rejected because the arbitrary-SHA surface remains). Reference this ADR from docs/releasing.md.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Narrow the 'release evidence' deliverable to fields that PEP 740 attestations do NOT already provide (approved SHA, version input, manifest versions, reviewer identity) and drop 'artifact hashes' from the list, since those are already cryptographically recorded by the existing `attestations: true` step at release.yml:73.
findings.finding: The plan's final What bullet proposes 'Record the approved SHA, version, manifest versions, and artifact hashes in release evidence,' but PEP 740 attestations are already active at release.yml:73 (`attestations: true` via `pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish`) and already produce cryptographic, signed, verifiable artifact hashes tied to this exact workflow run. Adding a separate manual 'artifact hashes' record rebuilds a weaker (unsigned) version of functionality the codebase already provides, violating NIH.
findings.location: What section — final bullet: 'Record the approved SHA, version, manifest versions, and artifact hashes in release evidence'; release.yml:73
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section final bullet, replace 'artifact hashes' with fields genuinely novel to this plan: 'Record the approved SHA, version input, manifest versions compared, and environment reviewer identity in release evidence (job summary or uploaded artifact).' Cross-reference the existing PEP 740 attestations for per-artifact hash provenance.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the integrity posture for the `mcp-publisher` binary download in the redesigned `release.yml`: either pin to an exact version URL + SHA-256 digest check before execution (preferred), or explicitly document and accept the floating-`latest` risk — the plan must not leave this posture undeclared on the OIDC-privileged `mcp_registry` job.
findings.finding: The `mcp_registry` job carries `id-token: write` (the OIDC token that authorizes publication to the MCP Registry) and downloads the `mcp-publisher` binary via a mutable `releases/latest` floating URL with no version pin and no SHA-256 digest check before execution. The plan redesigns `release.yml` but is silent on whether the new workflow will pin or integrity-verify this binary, leaving an undeclared integrity posture on a boundary-crossing execution path: a substituted or compromised binary could exfiltrate the OIDC token or publish malicious registry content.
findings.location: What section — `mcp_registry` job; release.yml:127-129 (current); plan Scope: `.github/workflows/release.yml`
findings.suggested_fix: In the new `release.yml` `mcp_registry` job, replace the `releases/latest` curl with a pinned version URL (e.g. `releases/download/v1.2.3/...`) and add a `sha256sum --check` step using a hardcoded expected digest before invoking the binary. Record the pinned version and digest in `docs/releasing.md` alongside a note to update them on each `mcp-publisher` upgrade.

findings.finding: The two-phase relay mechanism — dispatch job pushes the v* tag using the default GITHUB_TOKEN, that tag push triggers the existing build/publish jobs — is broken by GitHub's loop-prevention policy: pushes made with GITHUB_TOKEN from within a workflow do NOT trigger new workflow runs (GitHub explicitly excludes `push`-triggered workflows from GITHUB_TOKEN-created events to prevent infinite loops). If the dispatch job uses GITHUB_TOKEN to push the tag, the `push: tags: v*` trigger will never fire and `build`/`publish`/`github_release`/`mcp_registry` will never run.
findings.location: What — Two-phase flow; AC3 ('that workflow-created tag push triggers build/publish')
findings.suggested_fix: In the dispatch job's tag-creation step, push the tag with a PAT (e.g. `secrets.RELEASE_PAT`) or a GitHub App installation token rather than the implicit GITHUB_TOKEN. Add a note to `docs/releasing.md` that this secret must be provisioned as a one-time setup step (similar to the existing PyPI pending-publisher setup). Update AC3 to specify the token mechanism.

findings.finding: The two-phase flow's core mechanism — the dispatch job pushes a v* tag using the built-in GITHUB_TOKEN, and that push fires a new push:tags workflow run — is an unverified capability assertion. GitHub Actions explicitly does NOT trigger new workflow runs from events created by the GITHUB_TOKEN (documented loop-prevention). The dispatch job pushing a tag with GITHUB_TOKEN would silently produce no downstream build/publish run, making the entire two-phase design inoperative without a PAT or deploy-key credential for the tag-push step.
findings.location: What section — 'Two-phase flow' bullet; AC line 3: 'The dispatch job creates and pushes the v* tag only after the version-lockstep and ancestry checks pass; that workflow-created tag push is what triggers build/publish/github_release/mcp_registry'
findings.suggested_fix: Options in order of minimal-new-secret overhead: (1) use `gh workflow run release.yml --ref vX.Y.Z` from the dispatch job instead of relying on push-event re-trigger (single workflow, no re-trigger needed); (2) push the tag via the existing Gerrit deploy key if it has tag-write access (it already can write refs); (3) use a GitHub App installation token scoped to `contents:write` (no long-lived PAT). Document the chosen approach in the 'Two-phase flow' bullet and in the relevant AC.

findings.finding: The two-phase relay's core mechanism — the dispatch job pushes a v* tag and that tag push triggers the existing build/publish/github_release/mcp_registry jobs via `push: tags: v*` — is broken by GitHub's loop-prevention policy. GitHub explicitly suppresses new workflow runs triggered by push events when the push is made using the built-in `GITHUB_TOKEN`; the tag is created but no downstream workflow run fires. The plan never names the credential the dispatch job will use for the tag push, never acknowledges this GitHub platform constraint, and never states an auth precondition (PAT with `contents: write`, SSH deploy key, or alternative mechanism) for this specific new integration surface. The existing repo has `permissions: contents: read` at the release.yml workflow level, compounding the gap.
findings.location: What — Two-phase flow section; AC3 ('that workflow-created v* tag push triggers build/publish/github_release/mcp_registry')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies the mcp_registry job correctly derives its version from the dispatch version input (not from a tag ref) and that the MCP registry submission uses this value — verified against a staging/dry-run target, not production.
findings.finding: The 'What' section states 'The MCP job derives the version from the dispatch input (not from a tag),' implying the mcp_registry job is retained and modified to use the dispatch version input instead of the tag ref. This is a concrete code change to release.yml, but no AC verifies that the mcp_registry job works correctly under the new dispatch flow (correct version propagation, successful registry submission, or that it no longer depends on a tag trigger).
findings.location: What section, 'Tag + GitHub Release created AFTER…' bullet; Acceptance Criteria AC1–AC8; Scope 'release.yml' bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the canonical inventory of version sources (all field paths the lockstep check covers) in `docs/releasing.md` or an inline comment in `release.yml`, and note that any addition of a new version field to `server.json` or a new manifest MUST be accompanied by an update to the lockstep check — so the rule is visibly maintained, not silently stale.
findings.finding: The version-lockstep check hardcodes specific JSON/TOML field paths (`pyproject.toml:version`, `server.json.version`, `server.json.packages[].version`). The plan does not flag these paths as a rule-to-maintain or provide any guidance for updating the check if `server.json`'s schema evolves (e.g., new version fields are added, the `packages[]` array structure changes, or a new manifest file is introduced). A schema change would leave the check silently incomplete — still passing while producing a version mismatch in a newly added field.
findings.location: What section — 'Version-lockstep, before build (pydantic pattern)' bullet; AC line 2
findings.suggested_fix: In release.yml, add a comment block immediately above the version-check step listing all checked fields and stating 'Add new version fields here'. Mirror this list in docs/releasing.md under a 'Version sources' subsection so it is findable without reading the workflow YAML.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py` to the Scope section and specify that the `test_module_is_main_only_no_tag_lock` comment on line 42 must be updated to reflect that tags are left open for record-tagging (post-publish output), not for driving publication.
findings.finding: The plan's Scope section does not list `tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py` as needing an update, but the test's inline comment on line 42 (`# tags stay human-pushable (release.yml v* pushes) -> NO tag lock ruleset`) embeds the v*-tag-push rationale that this plan removes. After implementation, the assertion still passes but the stated reason is wrong, creating a misleading contract comment for future readers and a stale rationale that points at a removed publish path.
findings.location: Scope section; tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py:42
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section add: '`tests/unit/test_mirror_lock_terraform.py` — update the inline comment in `test_module_is_main_only_no_tag_lock` from "tags stay human-pushable (release.yml v* pushes)" to "tags stay human-pushable; after this story, the v* tag is a post-publish record output, not a publication trigger".' No assertion change is needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add permalink citations (GitHub workflow file URLs + relevant line ranges, or a specific release tag) for the uv and pydantic prior-art patterns cited in the 'What' section, so the attributed designs can be independently verified by implementers and reviewers.
findings.finding: The plan names 'uv's proven release process' and 'pydantic's pre-build version check' as the specific prior-art patterns being adopted, but supplies no URLs, workflow-file paths, or version/tag citations to let implementers or reviewers verify these attributions. An implementer cannot confirm whether uv actually implements workflow_dispatch + required-reviewers environment in the shape described, or whether pydantic's check is truly a pre-build step (rather than inside the build hook), making these unverified capability assertions about external projects.
findings.location: What section — 'Adopt uv's proven release process and pydantic's pre-build version check'; '(pydantic pattern)' bullet label; header paragraph 'Re-anchor the release to the proven OSS pattern (uv model)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section first paragraph, replace the bare names with links, e.g.: 'uv's proven release process (e.g. https://github.com/astral-sh/uv/blob/main/.github/workflows/release.yml)' and 'pydantic's pre-build version check (e.g. https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/blob/main/.github/workflows/ci.yml — the check-version job)'. If the exact workflow file paths have changed, link to the commit SHA rather than a mutable branch ref.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
